lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Calpurnia!

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Peace, ho! Caesar speaks.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Calpurnia!

lines.speaker: CALPURNIA
lines.text: Here, my lord.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Stand you directly in Antonius’ way,

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: When he doth run his course. Antonius!

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Caesar, my lord?

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Forget not, in your speed, Antonius,

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: To touch Calpurnia; for our elders say,

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: The barren, touched in this holy chase,

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Shake off their sterile curse.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: I shall remember:

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: When Caesar says “do this,” it is perform’d.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Set on; and leave no ceremony out.

lines.speaker: SOOTHSAYER
lines.text: Caesar!

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Ha! who calls?

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Bid every noise be still: peace yet again!

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Who is it in the press that calls on me?

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: I hear a tongue, shriller than all the music,

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Cry “Caesar!” Speak; Caesar is turn’d to hear.

lines.speaker: SOOTHSAYER
lines.text: Beware the ides of March.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: What man is that?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: A soothsayer bids you beware the ides of March.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Set him before me; let me see his face.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Fellow, come from the throng; look upon Caesar.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: What say’st thou to me now? speak once again.

lines.speaker: SOOTHSAYER
lines.text: Beware the ides of March.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: He is a dreamer; let us leave him: pass.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Will you go see the order of the course?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Not I.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: I pray you, do.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I am not gamesome: I do lack some part

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Of that quick spirit that is in Antony.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Let me not hinder, Cassius, your desires;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I’ll leave you.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Brutus, I do observe you now of late:

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: I have not from your eyes that gentleness

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And show of love as I was wont to have:

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: You bear too stubborn and too strange a hand

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Over your friend that loves you.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Cassius,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Be not deceived: if I have veil’d my look,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I turn the trouble of my countenance

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Merely upon myself. Vexed I am

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Of late with passions of some difference,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Conceptions only proper to myself,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Which give some soil perhaps to my behaviors;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: But let not therefore my good friends be grieved—

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Among which number, Cassius, be you one—

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Nor construe any further my neglect,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Than that poor Brutus, with himself at war,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Forgets the shows of love to other men.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Then, Brutus, I have much mistook your passion;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: By means whereof this breast of mine hath buried

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Thoughts of great value, worthy cogitations.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Tell me, good Brutus, can you see your face?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: No, Cassius; for the eye sees not itself,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: But by reflection, by some other things.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: ’Tis just:

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And it is very much lamented, Brutus,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: That you have no such mirrors as will turn

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Your hidden worthiness into your eye,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: That you might see your shadow. I have heard,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Where many of the best respect in Rome,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Except immortal Caesar, speaking of Brutus

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And groaning underneath this age’s yoke,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Have wish’d that noble Brutus had his eyes.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Into what dangers would you lead me, Cassius,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: That you would have me seek into myself

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: For that which is not in me?

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Therefore, good Brutus, be prepared to hear:

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And since you know you cannot see yourself

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: So well as by reflection, I, your glass,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Will modestly discover to yourself

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: That of yourself which you yet know not of.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And be not jealous on me, gentle Brutus:

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Were I a common laugher, or did use

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: To stale with ordinary oaths my love

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: To every new protester; if you know

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: That I do fawn on men and hug them hard

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And after scandal them, or if you know

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: That I profess myself in banqueting

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: To all the rout, then hold me dangerous.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: What means this shouting? I do fear, the people

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Choose Caesar for their king.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Ay, do you fear it?

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Then must I think you would not have it so.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I would not, Cassius; yet I love him well.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: But wherefore do you hold me here so long?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: What is it that you would impart to me?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: If it be aught toward the general good,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Set honour in one eye and death i’ the other,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And I will look on both indifferently,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: For let the gods so speed me as I love

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: The name of honour more than I fear death.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: I know that virtue to be in you, Brutus,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: As well as I do know your outward favour.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Well, honour is the subject of my story.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: I cannot tell what you and other men

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Think of this life; but, for my single self,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: I had as lief not be as live to be

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: In awe of such a thing as I myself.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: I was born free as Caesar; so were you:

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: We both have fed as well, and we can both

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Endure the winter’s cold as well as he:

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: For once, upon a raw and gusty day,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: The troubled Tiber chafing with her shores,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Caesar said to me “Darest thou, Cassius, now

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Leap in with me into this angry flood,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And swim to yonder point?” Upon the word,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Accoutred as I was, I plunged in

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And bade him follow; so indeed he did.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: The torrent roar’d, and we did buffet it

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: With lusty sinews, throwing it aside

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And stemming it with hearts of controversy;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: But ere we could arrive the point proposed,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Caesar cried “Help me, Cassius, or I sink!”

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: I, as Aeneas, our great ancestor,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Did from the flames of Troy upon his shoulder

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: The old Anchises bear, so from the waves of Tiber

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Did I the tired Caesar. And this man

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Is now become a god, and Cassius is

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: A wretched creature and must bend his body,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: If Caesar carelessly but nod on him.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: He had a fever when he was in Spain,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And when the fit was on him, I did mark

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: How he did shake: ’tis true, this god did shake:

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: His coward lips did from their colour fly,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And that same eye whose bend doth awe the world

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Did lose his lustre: I did hear him groan:

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Ay, and that tongue of his that bade the Romans

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Mark him and write his speeches in their books,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Alas, it cried “Give me some drink, Titinius,”

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: As a sick girl. Ye gods, it doth amaze me

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: A man of such a feeble temper should

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: So get the start of the majestic world

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And bear the palm alone.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Another general shout!

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I do believe that these applauses are

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: For some new honours that are heap’d on Caesar.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Why, man, he doth bestride the narrow world

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Like a Colossus, and we petty men

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Walk under his huge legs and peep about

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: To find ourselves dishonourable graves.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Men at some time are masters of their fates:

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: The fault, dear Brutus, is not in our stars,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: But in ourselves, that we are underlings.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Brutus and Caesar: what should be in that “Caesar”?

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Why should that name be sounded more than yours?

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Write them together, yours is as fair a name;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Sound them, it doth become the mouth as well;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Weigh them, it is as heavy; conjure with ’em,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Brutus will start a spirit as soon as Caesar.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Now, in the names of all the gods at once,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Upon what meat doth this our Caesar feed,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: That he is grown so great? Age, thou art shamed!

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Rome, thou hast lost the breed of noble bloods!

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: When went there by an age, since the great flood,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: But it was famed with more than with one man?

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: When could they say till now, that talk’d of Rome,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: That her wide walls encompass’d but one man?

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Now is it Rome indeed and room enough,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: When there is in it but one only man.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: O, you and I have heard our fathers say,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: There was a Brutus once that would have brook’d

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: The eternal devil to keep his state in Rome

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: As easily as a king.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: That you do love me, I am nothing jealous;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: What you would work me to, I have some aim:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: How I have thought of this and of these times,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I shall recount hereafter; for this present,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I would not, so with love I might entreat you,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Be any further moved. What you have said

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I will consider; what you have to say

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I will with patience hear, and find a time

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Both meet to hear and answer such high things.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Till then, my noble friend, chew upon this:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Brutus had rather be a villager

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Than to repute himself a son of Rome

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Under these hard conditions as this time

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Is like to lay upon us.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: I am glad that my weak words

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Have struck but thus much show of fire from Brutus.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: The games are done and Caesar is returning.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: As they pass by, pluck Casca by the sleeve;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And he will, after his sour fashion, tell you

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: What hath proceeded worthy note to-day.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I will do so. But, look you, Cassius,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: The angry spot doth glow on Caesar’s brow,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And all the rest look like a chidden train:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Calpurnia’s cheek is pale; and Cicero

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Looks with such ferret and such fiery eyes

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: As we have seen him in the Capitol,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Being cross’d in conference by some senators.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Casca will tell us what the matter is.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Antonius!

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Caesar?

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Let me have men about me that are fat:

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Sleek-headed men and such as sleep o’ nights:

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Yond Cassius has a lean and hungry look;

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: He thinks too much: such men are dangerous.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Fear him not, Caesar; he’s not dangerous;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: He is a noble Roman and well given.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Would he were fatter! But I fear him not:

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Yet if my name were liable to fear,

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: I do not know the man I should avoid

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: So soon as that spare Cassius. He reads much;

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: He is a great observer and he looks

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Quite through the deeds of men; he loves no plays,

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: As thou dost, Antony; he hears no music;

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Seldom he smiles, and smiles in such a sort

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: As if he mock’d himself and scorn’d his spirit

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: That could be moved to smile at anything.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Such men as he be never at heart’s ease

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Whiles they behold a greater than themselves,

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: And therefore are they very dangerous.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: I rather tell thee what is to be fear’d

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Than what I fear; for always I am Caesar.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Come on my right hand, for this ear is deaf,

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: And tell me truly what thou think’st of him.

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: You pull’d me by the cloak; would you speak with me?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Ay, Casca; tell us what hath chanced to-day,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: That Caesar looks so sad.

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Why, you were with him, were you not?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I should not then ask Casca what had chanced.

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Why, there was a crown offered him: and being offered him, he

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: put it by with the back of his hand, thus; and then

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: the people fell a-shouting.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: What was the second noise for?

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Why, for that too.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: They shouted thrice: what was the last cry for?

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Why, for that too.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Was the crown offered him thrice?

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Ay, marry, was’t, and he put it by thrice, every time gentler

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: than other, and at every putting-by mine honest neighbours shouted.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Who offered him the crown?

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Why, Antony.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Tell us the manner of it, gentle Casca.

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: I can as well be hanged as tell the manner of it:

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: it was mere foolery; I did not mark it. I saw Mark

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Antony offer him a crown;—yet ’twas not a crown neither, ’twas one

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: of these coronets;—and, as I told you, he put it by once:

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: but, for all that, to my thinking, he would fain have had

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: it. Then he offered it to him again; then he put it

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: by again: but, to my thinking, he was very loath to lay

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: his fingers off it. And then he offered it the third time;

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: he put it the third time by: and still as he refused

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: it, the rabblement hooted and clapped their chopped hands and threw up

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: their sweaty night-caps and uttered such a deal of stinking breath because

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Caesar refused the crown that it had almost choked Caesar; for he

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: swounded and fell down at it: and for mine own part, I

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: durst not laugh, for fear of opening my lips and receiving the

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: bad air.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: But, soft, I pray you: what, did Caesar swound?

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: He fell down in the market-place, and foamed at mouth, and was speechless.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: ’Tis very like: he hath the failing sickness.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: No, Caesar hath it not; but you and I

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And honest Casca, we have the falling sickness.

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: I know not what you mean by that; but, I am sure,

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Caesar fell down. If the tag-rag people did not clap him and

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: hiss him, according as he pleased and displeased them, as they use

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: to do the players in the theatre, I am no true man.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: What said he when he came unto himself?

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Marry, before he fell down, when he perceived the common herd was

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: glad he refused the crown, he plucked me ope his doublet and

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: offered them his throat to cut. An I had been a man

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: of any occupation, if I would not have taken him at a

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: word, I would I might go to hell among the rogues. And

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: so he fell. When he came to himself again, he said, If

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: he had done or said anything amiss, he desired their worships to

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: think it was his infirmity. Three or four wenches, where I stood,

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: cried “Alas, good soul!” and forgave him with all their hearts: but

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: there’s no heed to be taken of them: if Caesar had stabbed

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: their mothers, they would have done no less.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And after that, he came, thus sad, away?

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Ay.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Did Cicero say anything?

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Ay, he spoke Greek.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: To what effect?

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Nay, an I tell you that, I’ll ne’er look you i’ the

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: face again: but those that understood him smiled at one another and

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: shook their heads; but, for mine own part, it was Greek to

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: me. I could tell you more news too: Marullus and Flavius, for

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: pulling scarfs off Caesar’s images, are put to silence. Fare you well.

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: There was more foolery yet, if I could remember it.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Will you sup with me to-night, Casca?

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: No, I am promised forth.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Will you dine with me to-morrow?

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Ay, if I be alive and your mind hold and your dinner worth the eating.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Good: I will expect you.

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Do so. Farewell, both.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: What a blunt fellow is this grown to be!

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: He was quick mettle when he went to school.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: So is he now in execution

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Of any bold or noble enterprise,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: However he puts on this tardy form.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: This rudeness is a sauce to his good wit,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Which gives men stomach to digest his words

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: With better appetite.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And so it is. For this time I will leave you:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: To-morrow, if you please to speak with me,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I will come home to you; or, if you will,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Come home to me, and I will wait for you.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: I will do so: till then, think of the world.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Well, Brutus, thou art noble; yet, I see,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Thy honourable metal may be wrought

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: From that it is disposed: therefore it is meet

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: That noble minds keep ever with their likes;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: For who so firm that cannot be seduced?

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Caesar doth bear me hard; but he loves Brutus:

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: If I were Brutus now and he were Cassius,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: He should not humour me. I will this night,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: In several hands, in at his windows throw,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: As if they came from several citizens,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Writings all tending to the great opinion

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: That Rome holds of his name; wherein obscurely

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Caesar’s ambition shall be glanced at:

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And after this let Caesar seat him sure;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: For we will shake him, or worse days endure.

stage_directions.text: Flourish. Enter Caesar; Antony, for the course; Calpurnia, Portia, Decius, Cicero, Brutus, Cassius, and Casca; a great crowd following, among them a Soothsayer.
Sennet. Exeunt all except Brutus and Cassius.
Sennet. Exeunt Caesar and all his Train, but Casca.

glosses.definition: run the Lupercalia ceremonial race through the streets

glosses.phrase: barren, touched in this holy chase
glosses.definition: childless women struck by a Lupercalian runner's goatskin thong were believed to gain fertility
infertility, imagined as a divine affliction
go forward; proceed
omit none of the ritual observances
crowd; throng
March 15 by the Roman calendar; the Ides were the 15th of March, May, July, and October, the 13th of other months — a date for public business and, here, for assassination
the play's most famous prophecy; the Soothsayer's warning of the assassination date
the formal ceremony of the Lupercalian foot-race
playful; fond of sport or games

glosses.phrase: too stubborn and too strange a hand
glosses.definition: too cold and distant a manner
thoughts belonging only to me; private concerns
interpret; read meaning into
important reflections; weighty thoughts
the most respected men in Rome
the servitude of the present time — under Caesar
could see (this situation rightly); i.e. would resist Caesar's rise
a cheap jester; one who befriends everyone indiscriminately
cheapen by overusing common oaths of friendship
disorderly crowd; rabble
impartially; with equal regard for both
would just as soon; would prefer
the Tiber river rubbing angrily against its banks — the river flooding in winter
fully armed and clothed as I was
the legendary Trojan hero, mythical founder of Rome; subject of Virgil's *Aeneid*. He carried his father Anchises on his back when Troy fell
the burning of Troy by the Greeks — the event that drove Aeneas to Italy
Aeneas's aged father, whom he carried out of burning Troy on his shoulders

glosses.phrase: fever when he was in Spain
glosses.definition: Caesar's historical illness during his campaigns in Spain — Plutarch reports it; Cassius uses it to diminish Caesar's divine image
Cassius's lieutenant, later present at Philippi
gain advantage over; outstrip
take the prize (victor's palm-branch) by himself
straddle the world like a giant astride it
the bronze statue of Helios at Rhodes, over 100 feet tall; ancient wonder of the world; Cassius imagines it bestriding the harbour (a later legend not actually described by ancient sources)

glosses.phrase: fault, dear Brutus, is not in our stars
glosses.definition: blame lies not in fate but in human character — rejecting astrological determinism in favour of human agency
subordinates; inferiors
use as a magic-word to summon spirits
the primordial deluge, here the Roman/Greek version in which Deucalion and Pyrrha repopulate the world
pun — 'Rome' and 'room' were near-homophones in Elizabethan pronunciation; Cassius says Rome has shrunk to contain one man
Lucius Junius Brutus, the legendary founder of the Roman Republic (509 BCE) who expelled the last king Tarquinius Superbus; Marcus Brutus's ancestor
think it over; ruminate
grab Casca's sleeve to stop him
scolded attendants
sharp and red — like a ferret's eyes
Caesar's famous description of Cassius — gaunt, hungry for advantage; the line is lifted almost directly from North's Plutarch
well-disposed; of good character
lean; sparing
Caesar's formal self-naming — speaking of himself in the third person, in the regal style
outer garment; the Elizabethan cloak is an anachronism for a Roman toga
happened
(they) started shouting
yes, indeed (mild oath, from 'by the Virgin Mary')
lesser crowns; circlets — not full royal crowns
gladly; willingly
mob; the riff-raff
chapped, roughened working-class hands
swooned; fainted (Caesar's epileptic seizure)
epilepsy — the Elizabethan name; Plutarch records Caesar's attacks
Cassius's pun: Rome itself is in political decline ('falling') under Caesar
the ragged mob; the common people dismissively named
a close-fitting Elizabethan jacket — anachronism; a Roman would wear a toga, not a doublet. Casca's costume vocabulary is Renaissance, not ancient
Cicero actually spoke Greek (a learned language among Roman elites); Casca's 'Greek to me' is the origin of the modern idiom
unintelligible (to me) — the phrase enters English from this line
removed from office and silenced — likely executed or exiled; Plutarch says they were deprived of their tribuneship
have an evening meal
sharp in wit; spirited (metal/mettle pun)
roughness of manner (not modern 'rudeness')
noble character can be shaped (like metal); Cassius plans to seduce Brutus through forged letters
different handwritings — to disguise a single author
Caesar's desire for power will be hinted at; 'ambition' in Elizabethan usage is a political vice, heavier in weight than modern 'ambition'

annotations.title: The Soothsayer at the edge of the stage
annotations.body:
The Soothsayer's two-line interruption of the Lupercalian procession is dramatic compression at full strength. The prophecy arrives in fifteen words, and the play's entire clock is now set: every scene between this one and 3.1 counts down to the ides.

Shakespeare also uses the Soothsayer to characterise Caesar. Caesar's immediate response — 'He is a dreamer; let us leave him: pass' — is superstition-inflected (he takes the warning seriously enough to stop the procession) but finally dismissive. The play will revisit this exact psychological posture in 2.2: Caesar will take Calpurnia's dreams and the augurers' failed sacrifice seriously enough to stay home, then be argued out of his fear. The Soothsayer's warning is the first instance of a pattern that will kill him.
sources: John D. Cox, *Shakespeare and the Dramaturgy of Power* (1989)

annotations.title: Cassius's temptation scene: Shakespeare's template
annotations.body:
Cassius's long seduction of Brutus is the template for every later Shakespeare temptation scene. Iago seducing Othello (3.3), the Weird Sisters seducing Macbeth (1.3), Goneril and Regan drawing Lear, even Prospero dismissing Caliban — each owes its shape to Cassius here. The technique is specific: use what the target already half-believes, flatter him with mirror-images of his own virtue, supply the specific fear that rationalises the action the tempter wants.

Cassius takes Brutus's reverence for republican institutions ('Rome indeed and room enough / When there is in it but one only man') and weaponises the family name ('There was a Brutus once that would have brook'd / The eternal devil to keep his state in Rome / As easily as a king'). By the end of this scene, Brutus hasn't agreed to kill Caesar — but he has agreed to consider it, which is the movement the tempter needed.

Cassius's final soliloquy (L287–301) confirms the technique is conscious: 'Well, Brutus, thou art noble; yet, I see, / Thy honourable metal may be wrought / From that it is disposed.' The forged letters in Brutus's window are already planned.
sources: Harold Bloom, *Shakespeare: The Invention of the Human* (1998)

annotations.title: 'A lean and hungry look' — Plutarch in Shakespeare's mouth
annotations.body:
Caesar's assessment of Cassius is among the clearest examples of Shakespeare working directly from Sir Thomas North's 1579 translation of Plutarch's *Lives*. North's text reads: 'Caesar also had Cassius in great jealousy, and suspected him much: whereupon he said on a time to his friends, What will Cassius do, think ye? I like not his pale looks.'

Plutarch's *Life of Caesar* continues more broadly: 'As for those pale-visaged and carrion-lean people, I never reckon of them. But those fat men and smooth-combed heads, quoth he, I never reckon them dangerous. But these pale, visaged and carrion-lean people, I fear them most — meaning Brutus and Cassius.' Shakespeare has compressed this into the famous verse while keeping North's specific language: 'pale', 'lean', 'fear'.

This is Shakespeare's working method for *Julius Caesar*. The direct source is not Roman history in the abstract but one book on one shelf — Sir Thomas North's 1579 English translation of Jacques Amyot's 1559 French translation of Plutarch's Greek. The rhetorical moments the audience remembers ('Et tu, Brute', 'Beware the ides of March', 'lean and hungry look') are almost all places where Shakespeare lightly versifies North.
sources: Sir Thomas North, *Plutarch's Lives* (1579), 'Life of Caesar' §62
C. B. R. Pelling, 'Plutarch's Adaptation of His Source-Material', *JHS* 100 (1980)

annotations.title: Doublets in Rome: the play's anachronisms as policy
annotations.body:
Casca's report that Caesar 'pluck'd me ope his doublet' and 'offered them his throat to cut' is a casual line with a very un-casual costume. Doublets are Renaissance garments; a Roman would wear a toga and tunic. Shakespeare's Rome is full of these anachronisms: the clock that strikes in 2.1, the book with turning pages Brutus reads in 4.3, the mechanical clock again in 2.2 ('strucken eight'), the Cheapside cobbler's jokes in 1.1, Cassius's doublet in 1.3 ('unbraced').

These are not carelessness. Shakespeare's plays knew what Roman dress looked like — Julius Caesar had been a staple of Elizabethan history for a generation. The anachronisms are a deliberate strategy: Rome is made legible to the English audience by being dressed in their own clothes. The argument about republican politics, about the line between lawful authority and tyranny, about the body politic — all of these are easier to prosecute on a London stage when the Forum looks a bit like Westminster and the senators could, physically, be privy councillors.

The reader should not try to correct the anachronisms away. They are the mechanism by which the play does its political work on its audience.
sources: Naomi Conn Liebler, *Shakespeare's Festive Tragedy* (1995), ch. 4

annotations.title: Colossus and stars: the speech that invents Cassius's politics
annotations.body:
'Why, man, he doth bestride the narrow world / Like a Colossus' is the play's first formalised image of Caesar as over-scaled man. The Colossus of Rhodes (a hundred-foot bronze statue of Helios) was one of the Seven Wonders; the legend that it bestrode the harbour is medieval, not ancient, but had entered the English imaginary. Cassius translates the image into explicit anti-tyranny: the Colossus stands so huge that other men 'walk under his huge legs and peep about / To find ourselves dishonourable graves.'

The complement arrives immediately: 'The fault, dear Brutus, is not in our stars, / But in ourselves, that we are underlings.' This rejection of astrological determinism is itself political — if Caesar's rise is fated, it cannot be resisted; if it is merely a consequence of other men's cowardice, then it demands action. Cassius is building Brutus a philosophy of agency; the conspiracy is its necessary conclusion.

Shakespeare will replay the irony in 3.1 when Caesar declares himself 'constant as the northern star' immediately before being stabbed twenty-three times. The play is arguing something specific about the relation between a man's self-image and his fate.
sources: Ernst Cassirer, *The Myth of the State* (1946)
Wayne A. Rebhorn, 'The Crisis of the Aristocracy in *Julius Caesar*', *Renaissance Quarterly* 43 (1990)

trials.prompt: Where does Shakespeare get 'a lean and hungry look'?
options: From North's 1579 English translation of Plutarch, which he compresses almost verbatim

trials.prompt: Cassius's long seduction of Brutus functions as:
options: The template for later Shakespearean temptation scenes — Iago/Othello, the Witches/Macbeth all follow its shape
A flattering portrait of Cassius's eloquence without further purpose

trials.prompt: 'The fault, dear Brutus, is not in our stars, / But in ourselves, that we are underlings' is rhetorically aimed at:
options: Rejecting fate in favour of human agency, so the conspiracy becomes a moral duty

trials.prompt: What does Casca say happened at the Lupercal ceremony?
options: Antony thrice offered Caesar a crown, which Caesar put by each time — then Caesar fell in a seizure

trials.prompt: Casca's mention of Caesar 'pluck'd me ope his doublet' is:
options: A deliberate Renaissance-in-Rome anachronism; doublets are Elizabethan garments, one of several such dressings-in-English of the Roman setting

trials.prompt: Cassius's 'bestride the narrow world / Like a Colossus' refers to:
options: The bronze statue of Helios at Rhodes, legendarily said to straddle the harbour — an image of overbearing scale that dishonours those walking beneath

trials.prompt: Why is the Soothsayer's warning dramatically important despite its brevity?
options: It shows Shakespeare's interest in magic
It sets the play's clock — every scene between 1.2 and 3.1 now counts down to the ides of March
It introduces a new character unnecessary to the plot

trials.prompt: What does Cassius's closing soliloquy reveal about his method?
options: He is uncertain whether to proceed
The seduction of Brutus was consciously engineered, and the forged letters that will arrive in Brutus's window are next
He plans to abandon the plot
